title: Day 04 — GitHub User Search
summary: Learn promises, async/await, and fetch; type API responses; use useEffect for side effects; and build a GitHub user search with loading, error, empty, and success states.

blocks.content: Static forms are not enough. Real apps talk to servers — user profiles, payments, notifications. Today you cross the boundary from UI-only React to API-driven React. You will fetch public GitHub user data, manage async UI states, and learn useEffect — the hook interviewers ask about in every mid-level loop.

blocks.title: Scope for Day 04
blocks.content: Use useState from Day 02, reusable components from Day 03. React Router and custom hooks come on Days 05–06. Fetch on button submit is fine today; debounced search-as-you-type comes Day 06.

Network calls are asynchronous. The browser sends a request and continues — your code must wait for the response without freezing the UI. Promises model that future value. async/await makes Promise chains readable.

blocks.text: Promises — a value that arrives later

blocks.content: A Promise is pending, then settles fulfilled or rejected. fetch returns a Promise<Response>. You chain .then() or use await inside async functions. In React you almost always use async/await in handlers or inner effect functions.

blocks.title: fetch does not throw on 404
blocks.content: A 404 response still resolves the Promise. You must check response.ok or response.status before treating the body as success.

fetch is built into modern browsers. No axios required for today's exercise. GitHub's public API needs no auth for basic user lookup — perfect for learning. Respect rate limits; do not hammer the API in a loop while debugging.
JSON from the wire is untyped. You describe the shape you expect and TypeScript helps when you access user.avatar_url or user.public_repos. Mark nullable API fields optional.

blocks.title: Start small
blocks.content: Type only fields you render today. Extend the interface as the UI grows — same approach as production teams reading OpenAPI specs incrementally.

Render computes JSX from props and state — it should be pure. Side effects (fetch, timers, subscriptions) run after paint in useEffect. Alternatively, fetch in a submit handler is also valid when the action is user-triggered.

blocks.title: Never async useEffect callback
blocks.content: useEffect(() => async () => { ... }) is wrong — async returns a Promise React ignores. Define async function load inside and call load().
API UI is a small state machine. At most one primary view should dominate: idle empty, loading spinner, error alert, or profile card. Senior engineers sketch this matrix before writing JSX.

items.text: Empty — no search yet, prompt user

blocks.content: Build a search page: username input, search button, and a result area that handles all four states. Use your Day 03 Button and TextInput. Type the API response. No React Router yet — single page is fine.

items.text: Controlled search input for GitHub username
Loading, error, empty, and success UI
Correct useEffect or submit handler — no render-time fetch

blocks.content: Symptom: tab freezes, endless network requests. Cause: useEffect without deps calls setState every render. Fix: add dependency array; fetch only when needed; guard empty username.
Symptom: stale username results or effect never updates. Cause: username missing from deps. Fix: [username] when effect reads username.
Symptom: old user flashes after new search. Cause: race between overlapping requests. Fix: cancelled flag or AbortController in effect cleanup.
